coredata.dc:title: Point-to-face assembled carbon architecture targeting photothermal and electrothermal synergetic conversion and management for efficient cleanup of crude-oil spills
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lin
Wang, Jiarong
Xue, Chaorui
Chang, Qing
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Porous materials capable of efficiently remediating crude oil spills are needed for resource and environmental sustainability. However, a big challenging is to achieve high adsorption capacity and speed owing to high viscosity and sluggish flowability of crude oils. We here report a novel hierarchical carbon architecture featuring zero-dimentional carbon dots assembled on reduced graphene oxide nanosheets upon commercial sponge. It exhibits extraordinary sorption capacity of 97.4 g/g and provides a highly modular photothermal and electrothermal conversion platform for rapidly heating crude oils with the minimum heat losses, enabling efficient adsorption of crude oils. Thanks to its excellent energy management ability, the energy efficiency for solar to crude oil adsorption reaches to 248 % with the aid of proper input voltage. Besides, the crude oil value recycled in one hour is 3.15 ∼ 4.43 times than the cost of materials. Therefore, this work offers a new material architecting system for addressing catastrophic large-area spills of crude oils.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous adsorption material
Carbon dots
Graphene
Heat management
Solar energy conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photothermal electrothermal test and crude oil adsorption

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province of China
Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province of China and Graduate Student Innovation Project of Shanxi Province of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank for financial support Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province of China (20210302123037), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022-136), Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province of China and Graduate Student Innovation Project of Shanxi Province of China (2022Y664).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographs showing mechanical strength and flexural toughness of MS-rGO-CDs; (b) Photographs for drop of water upon the surface of MS-rGO-CDs, MS-rGO, MS-CDs; (c) Water contact angles of MS-CDs, MS-rGO, and MS-rGO-CDs. (d-f) SEM images with different magnifications of MS-CDs; (g-i) SEM images with different magnifications of MS-rGO; (j-l) SEM images with different magnifications of MS-rGO-CDs.

Crude oil adsorption performances. (a and b) The effect of the mass ratios of CDs and GO on adsorption performances of crude oil; (c) Adsorption performance of MS-rGO-CDs under different conditions as labeled; (d) Adsorption performance comparison of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs under one sun; (e) The photographs showing electric conductivity properties of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs; (f) Adsorption performances of MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs applying the voltage of 10 and 20 V, respectively; (g) Comparison of the adsorption capacity of MS-rGO-CDs with the reported porous adsorbents with one sun irradiation; (h) Cyclic stability of MS-rGO-CDs; (i) Photographs showing hydrophobicity of MS-rGO-CDs in sorption–desorption processes.

(a) Absorption spectra of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs in the range of 200–2500 nm; (b) Horizontal and top view infrared images of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs; (c and d) Temperature curves of upper and lower surfaces of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs with time under one sun, respectively; (e) Surface temperature curve of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs with time under a voltage of 10 V applied; (f and g) Temperature curves of upper and lower surfaces of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs with time under the coupling of one sun and 10 V voltage, respectively; (h) The energy utilization efficiency of crude oil adsorption for MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs under one sun, applying 10 V voltage and their coupling.

(a) EIS spectra of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs; (b) Photocurrent responses of MS-CDs, MS-rGO, and MS-rGO-CDs under one sun; (c) Linear potential scans of MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs under one sun irradiation and dark condition, the insets are the enlarged view of the corresponding sections; (d) Simulated temperature distributions of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs with different heat transfer modes.
Mechanism illustration of photothermal and electrothermal conversion and heat transfer of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs.

head.ce:title: Point-to-face assembled carbon architecture targeting photothermal and electrothermal synergetic conversion and management for efficient cleanup of crude-oil spills

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hierarchical carbon architecture featuring carbon dots assembled on reduced graphene oxide nanosheets upon commercial sponge provides a highly modular photothermal and electrothermal conversion platform for heating crude oils to efficiently recycle.

Porous materials capable of efficiently remediating crude oil spills are needed for resource and environmental sustainability. However, a big challenging is to achieve high adsorption capacity and speed owing to high viscosity and sluggish flowability of crude oils. We here report a novel hierarchical carbon architecture featuring zero-dimentional carbon dots assembled on reduced graphene oxide nanosheets upon commercial sponge. It exhibits extraordinary sorption capacity of 97.4 g/g and provides a highly modular photothermal and electrothermal conversion platform for rapidly heating crude oils with the minimum heat losses, enabling efficient adsorption of crude oils. Thanks to its excellent energy management ability, the energy efficiency for solar to crude oil adsorption reaches to 248 % with the aid of proper input voltage. Besides, the crude oil value recycled in one hour is 3.15 ∼ 4.43 times than the cost of materials. Therefore, this work offers a new material architecting system for addressing catastrophic large-area spills of crude oils.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Serious crude oil spills often happen worldwide as a consequence of petroleum pipeline, oil tanker and offshore oil field accidents, causing severe ecological and environmental impacts as well as great economic damages.[1,2] Various technologies including dissipation with dispersants, combustion, boom isolation or skimming have developed for crude-oil remediation.[3–5] Nevertheless, these approaches usually suffer from not only additional negative environmental influences but also low adsorption efficiency and huge consumption of resource.[6,7] Porous adsorbent material is regarded an attractive alternative for oil spill cleanup because of their low cost and high sorption capacity. [8–10] Unfortunately, most of these adsorbents are inefficient for the recovery of crude oils with high viscosity (103 to 105 mPa‧s), which can tremendously obstruct oil diffusion in the channels of adsorbents.[3,11] Very recently, heatable porous materials, e.g. low-dimentional carbon or other photothemal nanomaterial modified sponges, have been emerging to use for the adsorption of crude oils by reducing their viscosity and increasing their fluidity via electric or photothermal heating.[10,12] Electric heating can realize a stable heat supply without incurring the effects of the weather, but has a potential risk on account of the high power applied.[10,13] Converting sunlight to heat crude oils in the site through photothermal porous materials can significantly save operation costs, while it still bears a low solar-to-heat conversion efficiency and a slow heat transfer rate.[5].

Limited by light penetration depth, the conversion of solar energy to heat is usually localized at the surface of porous adsorbent materials.[14] Fast heat transfer to the bottom crude oils with a minimum thermal loss is required for the effective decrease of the viscosity.[11] Therefore, heat management is also indispensable to improve the crude oil collection efficiency of photothermal porous materials.[15] Generally, graphene-based materials possess superiority in broad-band absorption from the visible to infrared range. However, they still confronts the challenge of high emissivity in the infrared range, leading to a huge thermal radiation loss. Accordingly, the overall utilization efficiency of solar-converted heat via graphene-based materials (e.g. in solar-driven interfacial water evaporation [16] and oil adsorption fields) often falls far behind expectations. Cheng et al. employed chemical doping based on the plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition technique to reduce graphene infrared emissivity for improving crude oil collecting efficiency, [17] but giving rise to an extremely high material and manufacturing cost. Moreover, loading metallic nanostructures on graphene-based materials can enhance photothermal utilization efficiency, but significantly lowers the adsorption capacity.[18] For instance, CuFeSe2-loaded graphene aerogel only exhibits an adsorption capacity of 18.63 g•g−1. [19] Thus, there is a great desire for the new material architecture with low-cost, high absorption capacity, efficient energy conversion and heat management for efficient cleanup of crude-oil spills.

We here present a hierarchical carbon architecture featuring low-cost carbon dots (CDs) assembled on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets upon commercial sponge. Thus, a highly modular photothermal and electrothermal conversion platform is developed for rapidly heating crude oils with the minimum heat losses. This platform allows for the synergy of electric heating and photothermal effect, dramatically enhancing energy conversion and utilization. More impressively, by controlling the loaded amount of CDs and rGOs can make maximum use of the pore volumes of sponge. Compared to the other heatable porous materials as reported, our presented hierarchical carbon architecture exhibits superior capacity (97.4 g•g−1) and higher rate of crude oil adsorption without incurring the burden of elemental scarcity or expensive materials and without the need for costly, stringent, tedious, or intricate fabrication steps. The energy efficiency for solar to crude oil adsorption reaches to 248 % with the aid of proper input voltage, realizing efficient use of solar energy in crude-oil remediation. Hence, this work provides new insights to design new self-heating system for rapidly collecting viscous crude oils in the offshore areas.

Commercial porous melamine sponge (MS) was purchased from Ledian Furniture Company of China without further treatment. Graphite powders, polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), and hydrogen peroxide (30 v/v%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagents. HI, formic acid (88 v/v%), concentrated sulfuric acid (98 wt%), concentrated phosphoric acid (85 wt%), anhydrous ethanol (AEA) and ethyl acetate (EA) were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory, China. All chemical reagents were directly used without further purification.

Commercial MS (20 × 20 × 5 mm) was firstly cleaned with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water, and dried at 60 ℃ for reserve. GOs were fabricated with natural graphite powders through the modified Hummers method.[20] Colloidal GO suspensions with different concentrations (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 mg‧mL−1) were prepared by deionized water dilution. After that, the porous MS was immersed in GO suspensions with different concentrations to be filled completely. Next, the resultant MS was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min to remove the excess GO suspension away from the pores, and then dried at 60 ℃ again to achieve MS-GO sponges with different GO loads. Of note, the loading amount of GO on MS was determined by the concentration of GO solution at the given centrifugal speed and time. Third, the MS-GO was reduced with the hot HI (≥45.0 wt%) solution keeping 95 ℃. The MS-GO first reacted with HI for 20 s and then further reacted for 20 s after reversing to ensure sufficient reaction. After washing 3 to 5 times with ethanol and water respectively, the sample was dried at 60 ℃ to obtain the MS-rGO.

CDs were prepared by selective oxidation etching of coal tar pitch with hydrogen peroxide and formic acid mixture as our reported approach. [21] CDs suspensions with different concentrations (0, 15, 30, 45, 60 and 75 mg‧mL−1) were prepared by dispersing different amounts of CDs in the same volume of anhydrous ethanol under ultrasonic agitation. Taking 25 mg of PDMS put into 25 mL EA under stirring to obtain PDMS-EA solution. Afterwards taking 5 mL of as-prepared CDs suspension was added into the obtained PDMS-EA solution. The resultant mixture and the MS-rGO together were transferred into the reactor and then kept at 150 ℃ for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, taking out the sponge and drying it at 60 ℃, the final samples of MS-rGO-CDs containing around 0, 4, 8, 12, 16 and 20 mg of CDs were gained, respectively. The loading amount of CDs on the resultant MS-rGO-CDs was confirmed to be in direct proportion to the concentrations of CD suspensions as prepared above based on weighting calculation method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal electrothermal test and crude oil adsorption
ce:section.ce:para:
Crude oil adsorption test: Artificial seawater (3.5 wt% NaCl solution) and crude oil were put into a beaker to get a mixture for simulating an accidental spill of crude oil. A solar simulator (CEL-HXUV300, China Education au light) with AM1.5G filter with an intensity of 100 mW cm−2 (one sun) was used to simulate natural sunlight exposure. Multivoltage steps were applied on the two ends of the samples through Voltage regulator (MS603D) to assess the electric heating adsorption performance. In order to quantitatively test the adsorption capacity of oil, the sponge sample was hung on a bracket connected to an electronic balance to monitor and record the change in the weight of the sponge sample in real time. The adsorption capacity of crude oil was calculated by the equation (1) [4]
(1)
G =
M ̇
- M
M
where G is the adsorption capacity of sample sponge (g‧g−1);
M ̇
is the mass of sample sponge after adsorbing crude oils (g); M is the initial mass of sample sponge (g).

Cyclic performance test: The sponge sample absorbed crude oils under one sun condition until it reached saturation, and the total amount of crude oil adsorption was recorded. The sponge was then manually squeezed to collect the oil. Let this sample repeatedly conduct the above adsorption and extrusion processes for multiple times.

Test of oil heat capacity: The differential thermal scanner (DSC) was used to test the heat flux of crude oil in the range of 23–100 ℃ in a rate of 8 ℃ min−1. The specific heat capacity of crude oil (cp) was calculated by the formula (2): [22]
(2)
c p
=
1 β
∙
dQ
m ∙ d t
where cp is the specific heat capacity of crude oil (J/g ℃-1);
dQ
m ∙ d t
is the heat flux of crude oil (W/g); β is the rate of warming (℃ s−1).

Surface temperature test: Infrared (IR) camera was used to record the temperature and thermal field distribution on the surface of the samples during the photothermal and/or electrothermal heating processes. The upper and lower surface temperatures of the sample were measured using a two-channel thermometer (Xinster HT-9815). The thermocouple wires directly contacted with the upper and lower surfaces of the samples to monitor temperature changes on the upper and lower surfaces in real time.

Fabrication and characterization of the hierarchical carbon architectures. As illustrated in Figure S1, the MS with a specific dimension was firstly filled with the GO suspension. The resultant MS was then centrifuged to remove the excess GO suspension away from MS pores. The residual amount of GO nanosheets on the surface of MS skeletons can be controlled by the concentration of GO suspension and centrifugal speed to a certain extent. Third, the GO coating was reduced with hot HI solution for in situ preparation rGO coating upon MS (hereafter, MS-rGO). Fourth, MS-rGO was immersed into the mixed solution of CDs, PDMS and EA (Figure S2, showing strong light emission under UV lamp) and further treated at 150 ℃ in a sealed Teflon-lined reactor. After reaction for 2 h, the hierarchical carbon architecture (named as MS-rGO-CDs) was achieved at last. For comparison, the MS without rGO coating was directly reacted with the mixed solution of CDs, PDMS and EA to prepare CDs-coated MS (MS-CDs) based on above method.

Typically, it can be observed that the color of the MS is gradually changed from white to dark black as the experimental steps proceed (Figure S3). By the characterization of Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy, the crosslinking networks of PDMS were formed on the surface of samples (Figure S4). As-prepared samples including MS-rGO-CDs are highly flexible and able to be contorted without damage (Fig. 1 a and Figure S5). Meanwhile, they are hydrophobic due to the coating of PDMS on the surface of samples (Fig. 1b). Water contact angle of three samples is very close because the same concentration of PDMS was employed during preparation process (Fig. 1c), indicating their consistent hydrophobic properties (Figure S6). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs show that the substrate MS remains open porous structures (Fig. 1d-l). Differently, we observed that CDs were assembled on the MS skeletons for MS-CDs (Fig. 1d-f), while GO nanosheets wrapped most of MS skeletons and occupied some of MS pores due to their large size for MS-GO (Fig. 1g-i), whereupon the unique hierarchical carbon architecture consisting of CDs and rGO was constructed in MS-rGO-CDs after the mixed solution of CDs, PDMS and EA reacted with MS-rGO (Fig. 1j-l). From Fig. 1l, the assemblies of CDs upon the surface of MS-rGO were observed clearly.

Oil-sorption performance. The lipophilic properties of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs were compared by dripping a drop of crude oil on their surface. The time required for a drop of oil to be completely absorbed was 8, 6, and 4 s for MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs, respectively, indicating the best lipophilic property of MS-rGO-CDs among three samples (Figure S7). A quantitative evaluation of the crude-oil sorption into samples was performed by weighting their mass changes with contact time. [8] The adsorption response of different samples on crude oils was firstly examined under one sun of irradiation. As the rGO-loaded amount increases in the MS-rGO-CDs, the crude-oil sorption increases initially and decreases afterwards (Fig. 2 a). This attributes to two reasons primarily: one results from the increase of sorbent weight according to the equation (1); the other is that the pores and channels in MS are possibly blocked by excessive photothermal agents. Analogously, the same phenomenon occurs when the CDs-loaded amount increases without changing the amount of rGO (Fig. 2b). As a result, the combination of rGO with CDs upon MS can significantly influence the solar-to-heat conversion and the sorption capacity of MS-rGO-CDs. The optimal ratio of CDs and rGO is equal to 1.

Fig. 2c shows the adsorption evolution of MS-rGO-CDs for crude oils with contact time under different conditions. The adsorption capacity of MS-rGO-CDs can reach saturation (97.4 g•g−1) in 10, 15 and 5 min under one sun irradiation, applying a voltage of 10 V and their coupling (Figure S8a), respectively. Hence, the speed of oil spill cleanup can be efficiently accelerated by applying voltage and solar irradiation simultaneously (Figure S9). Due to high viscosity of crude oils,[10] however, no any oil sorption occurs for MS-rGO-CDs in dark condition without any energy input (Fig. 2c and Figure S8b). For comparison, Fig. 2d provides the oil adsorption behaviors of MS-rGO and MS-CDs with contact time under one sun. Their adsorption capacities only attain 65 % and 75 % in 10 min, respectively even though they have the same loading mass of rGO or CDs upon MS with MS-rGO-CDs (Figure S10). As the higher solar intensity was inputted, the adsorption curve slopes of all samples became steeper at the initial stage (Figure S11), but the MS-rGO-CDs still exhibited the faster adsorption rate compared to both MS-rGO and MS-CDs. Meanwhile, three samples were also employed to perform outdoor contrast test as shown in Figure S12. Under natural sunlight irradiation, the MS-rGO-CDs takes the shortest time to reach oil-adsorption saturation among three samples. All of above results demonstrated that the efficiency in solar conversion and utilization of MS-rGO-CDs is far superior to that of MS-rGO and MS-CDs.

As illustrated in Fig. 2e, the MS-CDs possesses a high resistance, and it thereby is invariably unresponsive to crude oils while applying voltage from 10 to 20 V (Figure S13). The MS-rGO shows good electrical conductivity properties (Fig. 2e) and its electrical conductivity depends on the rGO amount coated on MS (Figure S14). As thus, we let MS-rGO have the same amount of rGO with MS-rGO-CDs to examine the dependence of their adsorption performance on input voltages. It can be found that MS-rGO-CDs can respond to crude oil at the lower voltage (10 V), while MS-rGO requires the higher voltage applied (20 V) for crude-oil sorption (Fig. 2f). Therefore, the assembly of CDs on the surface of rGO enables electrothermal conversion more efficiently. Most impressively, the oil sorption capacities of our presented MS-rGO-CDs, which is especially suitable for adsorption of highly viscous solvents (Figure S15), outperforms those of heatable porous adsorbents as reported [4,8,9,12,23–38] (Fig. 2g and Table S1). This makes the presented sorption material particularly interesting because there are greater advantages in low operation cost and highly efficient reclaim of crude oils. Besides, the stability of MS-rGO-CDs was explored by repeated use. It is clear that MS-rGO-CDs shows an only tiny decrease (∼5%) in adsorption capacity even after ten cycles (Fig. 2h) and still maintains robust hydrophobicity/oleophilicity (Fig. 2i).

We calculated the materials cost (CMS ) of this MS-rGO-CDs (Table S2). It is merely 9.07 $ m−2. Featured with its large sorption capacity and fast adsorption rate, the cost-effectiveness (Eoil ) is defined as,
(3)
E
Oil
=
ε ·
v
t 1
ρ
Oil
·
C
O i l
C
MS
·
t
t 1
=
ε v t
C
O i l
ρ
Oil
C
MS
where ε is the volume loss ratio during repeated adsorption (0.95); v is the adsorption rate of crude oils (kg m-2h−1); t1 represents the time required for oil adsorption saturation; ρOil and COil are the density (kg L-1) and international price (65 ∼ 85 $/bbl) of crude oils, respectively; t is the adsorption time. If t = 1 h, EOi
l = 315 % ∼ 443 %, indicating that the crude oil value recycled in 1 h is 3.15 ∼ 4.43 times than the cost of materials. Therefore, the MS-rGO-CDs provides a promising solution to achieve both high adsorption rate and good cost-effectiveness simultaneously.

Heat conversion and transfer efficiency. UV–Vis-NIR diffusive spectroscopy was firstly conducted to confirm sunlight harvesting ability of samples (Fig. 3 a and Figure S16). The MS-rGO-CDs shows a stronger light absorption overlapping the solar spectrum (200–––2500 nm) than MS-rGO (Fig. 3a). In contrast, UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectrum of MS-CDs evinces its relatively low absorbance, especially at near infrared region. This means there should be a lower solar-to-thermal conversion efficiency. As expected, the infrared images from lateral and top view reveal the different surface temperatures of three samples (Fig. 3b). With one sun of irradiation, the temperature changes at top and bottom surfaces with time were provided in Fig. 3c and d. The top surface temperatures of MS-CDs, MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs quickly rise to 62, 70 and 76 ℃, respectively. Surprisingly, the bottom surface temperature of MS-rGO was identical with that of MS-CDs. Since the larger temperature difference was generated between the top surface and the bottom surface of MS-rGO, the more solar-converted thermal energy on MS-rGO could be lost in the surrounding environment via heat conduction and thermal radiation compared with MS-CDs. Hence, the heat management ability of MS-rGO should be inferior to that of MS-CDs. In fact, this case is also verified by oil-sorption performance mentioned above.

As inputting a voltage on the samples, the temperature at top surface and bottom surface is consistent due to Joule heating effect. Fig. 3e illustrates a big difference of about 20 ℃ for MS-rGO and MS-rGO-CDs at a voltage of 10 V, reflecting a more efficient electrothermal conversion of MS-rGO-CDs. To examine the photothermal and electrothermal synergetic conversion, we further monitored the surface temperature evolution with time when exerting one sun of irradiation and a voltage of 10 V simultaneously. From Fig. 3f and g, the temperature gap between MS-rGO-CDs and the contrast samples, particularly at their bottom surface becomes even greater. This should be attributed to its superior heat conversion and management.[14,27] More obviously, the MS-rGO produces the maximal thermal gradient among three samples, confirming its inefficient heat management once again. As thus, the coating of CDs on the surface of MS-rGO could promote not only the photothermal and electrothermal synergetic conversion but also the converted heat utilization in oil adsorption. In addition, we quantitatively evaluated the energy efficiency (η) of solar to crude oil adsorption by the following equation (4),
(4)
η =
m
c p
Δ T
C
opt
q o
where m is the adsorption rate of crude oils (g cm-2s−1); cp is the specific heat capacity of crude oils (J/g ℃-1), which can be obtained by DSC measurements (Figure S17); △T (℃) is the temperature change of crude oils into the sample as given in Figure S18; C opt and q 0 represent the optical concentration and the solar intensity (100 mW cm−2), respectively. Fig. 3h shows the η of three samples under different heating conditions. Most impressively, the MS-rGO-CDs presents an extraordinarily high value no matter what in solar irradiation, applying voltage or their coupling. Its η even surpasses 100 % under one sun and 10 V and reaches to 248 %. This phenomenon should be resulted from the thermal energy reuse during heating crude oils with MS-rGO-CDs, and be also derived from its excellent downward heat transmission attributes thereby minimizing heat loss to surroundings.

Mechanism of photothermal conversion and heat management. It is well-known that the interband transition of electrons can induce visible and infrared emission for carbon-based nanomaterials,[17] resulting in energy loss. As for MS-rGO-CDs, this electron behavior in MS-rGO could be efficiently suppressed by the coating of CDs, which can separate photo- and/or electro-excited charges and transfer/accept their energy.[21] By electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), we provided Nyquist plots in Fig. 4 a, displaying an obviously reduced charge transfer resistance for MS-rGO-CDs compared to both MS-rGO and MS-CDs. To confirm it, we further recorded their corresponding photocurrent responses under one sun irradiation (Fig. 4b). The photocurrent signifies the occurrence of the interband transition of electrons in the samples excited by sunlight. [39] The largest photocurrent response from MS-rGO-CDs indicates that CDs could inhibit the radiation recombination of electrons through the efficient charge transfer process. To shed light on the contribution of the input voltage to the improvement of the thermal conversion and utilization, we conducted the linear potential scans shown in Fig. 4c. Phenomenally, the onset potential of MS-rGO-CDs was markedly lower than that of MS-rGO as the potential was applied in dark conditions, implying more easily electrothermal conversion in MS-rGO-CDs than MS-rGO. After coupling solar irradiation, their onset potentials further decreased due to photocurrent generation, thus probably enabling more Joule heat generation instead of radiation losses.

Heat transfer is the rate-limiting step of solar-driven crude oil adsorption. The MS is a kind of thermal insulation material.[40] The solar-converted heat via CDs assembled on MS could mainly transmit crude oils through thermal radiation.[17] Unlike with CDs, rGO nanosheets usually have a high thermal conductivity. Hence the solar-converted heat in MS-rGO could be dominated by heat conduction of rGO nanosheets upon the skeleton of MS. For MS-rGO-CDs, both thermal radiation and heat conduction could coexist for heating crude oils. Based on this, the solar-converted heat transmission processes was analyzed by computational simulation. The calculated results are provided in Fig. 4d. It can be found that the heat transmission efficiency of MS-rGO-CDs is remarkably higher than that of MS-rGO and MS-CDs. Moreover, the sample was heated by a hot plate with the same temperature. MS-rGO-CDs also displayed the smallest temperature difference between the top surface and the heated surface among three samples (Figure S19), matching with the calculated results.

Mechanism of photothermal and electrothermal synergetic conversion over MS-rGO-CDs. Fig. 5 illustrates our proposed mechanism of photothermal and electrothermal synergetic conversion and management over MS-rGO-CDs. The assembly of CDs on MS-rGO not only enhances solar energy capture, but also suppresses the visible and infrared emission from the interband transition of electrons through charge transfer processes, diminishing energy losses.[17] Applying a suitable voltage can further block radiation recombination through facilitating charge transfer and then boost heat conversion. Meanwhile, the delivery of solar-converted thermal energy to crude oils is also accelerated by cooperating thermal radiation of CDs with heat conduction of rGO, enhancing the oil-sorption speed. Consequently, the MS-rGO-CDs exhibits excellent ability to adsorb viscous crude oils under solar irradiation and proper input voltage, significantly outperforming MS-rGO and MS-CDs as well as the other oil sorbents as reported previously.

The hierarchical carbon architecture has been constructed through the assembly of CDs on rGO-wrapped MS. Implementation of it in oil-spill remediation exhibits extraordinary capacity and enables exceptionally fast adsorption of viscous crude oils under solar irradiation and proper input voltage. Besides the enhanced sunlight harvesting, the incorporation of CDs with rGO minimizes heat loss by suppressing the light radiation from the interband transition of electrons and rapidly transferring solar-converted thermal energy to crude oils. This will cause a significant decrease in oil viscosity and a dramatic increase in oil diffusion into the pores of MS-rGO-CDs, speeding up the oil-sorption rate. Since the entire materials and operation processes can be viable from an economic perspective, our hierarchical carbon architecture is expected for the application in practical cleanup of large-area viscous oil spills.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for financial support Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province of China (20210302123037), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022-136), Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province of China and Graduate Student Innovation Project of Shanxi Province of China (2022Y664).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.147185.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oil spill causes mass mortality of sea snakes in the Gulf of Oman
